coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence sensor based on surface functionalization and heteroatom co-doped carbon dots for detection of tetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Weifeng
Liang, Chuyao
Li, Yi
Shao, Cuicui
Zheng, Yanghuan
Li, Zhijian
Pu, Shouzhi
Fan, Congbin
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC), as a budget broad-spectrum antibiotic, its wide-ranging use might result in residues being left in the environment and existed potential harm. Hence, we designed a ratiometric fluorescence probe (N,P@CDs@Eu) based on Eu functionalized nitrogen and phosphorus elements in-situ self-doping carbon dots (CDs) for detection of TC. The N,P@CDs was synthesized utilizing adenosine triphosphate (ATP) contained a significant amount of phosphate groups. These phosphate groups served as binding sites for Eu, facilitating functionalization of N,P@CDs to form the N,P@CDs@Eu. Under ultraviolet irradiation, N,P@CDs@Eu only exhibited the intense emission at 440 nm. However, during the sensing process, TC quenched the fluorescence at 440 nm of via inner filter effect (IFE), and induced N,P@CDs@Eu to exhibited fluorescence at 617 nm through antenna effect (AE). So that, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detection of TC had been constructed, which could achieve quantitative determination within 0.1–50 μM range, the detection limit of 22.6 nM. Moreover, N,P@CDs@Eu exhibited outstanding detection capabilities of TC in milk and tap water, with high recovery rates (99.47 %–108.12 %) and low errors (R < 4.68 %), indicating N,P@CDs@Eu had enormous application prospects in the field of food safety and environmental detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Heteroatoms self-doping
Europium (Eu) Tetracycline (TC)
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detecting the sensing mechanism of TC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra, (b) XPS spectra of N,P@CDs (black curve) and N,P@CDs@Eu (red curve); XPS spectrum of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) P 2p of N,P@CDs.
(a) Fluorescence excitation, emission and UV–visible absorption spectra of N,P@CDs@Eu; (b) Fluorescence spectra of N,P@CDs@Eu with or without TC.

(a) The emission spectral change of the N,P@CDs@Eu in response to during 0 − 60 μM TC; (b) Fitting curves between F617/F440 of sensing system and concentration of TC; (c) Photographs of sensing solution with different TC concentrations under UV irradiation.
The change of the ratio of F617/F440 of N,P@CDs@Eu after adding different substances.

(a) Fluorescence excitation spectrum of N,P@CDs@Eu (red curve) and UV–vis spectrum of TC (black curve); (b) Fluorescence lifetime of N,P@CDs@Eu (with and without TC) at 440 nm; (c) UV–vis spectrum of various solutions; (d) The energy transfer pathway from TC to Eu.
the fabrication process of the N,P@CDs@Eu probe and its sensing mechanism with TC.
Fluorescence lifetime of N,P@CDs@Eu suspension with or without TC.
Practical application of detection TC by N,P@CDs@Eu.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence sensor based on surface functionalization and heteroatom co-doped carbon dots for detection of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, Institute of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, Institute of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By synthesizing a novel flourescence probe N,P@CDs via a facile and environmental-friendly method by utilizing adenosine triphosphate (ATP) as carbon source. The introduction of N and P elements could effectively improve the luminescence of CDs. Then, Eu was modified on the surface of N,P@CDs by post-modification synthesis technique to obtain the N,P@CDs@Eu complex. During the sensing process, TC quenched the fluorescence at 440 nm of via inner filter effect (IFE), and induced N,P@CDs@Eu to exhibited fluorescence at 617 nm through antenna effect (AE). By observing the ratio of emission peaks at 617 nm and 440 nm (F617/F440) and color changes, the TC content could be sensitively detected.

Tetracycline (TC), as a budget broad-spectrum antibiotic, its wide-ranging use might result in residues being left in the environment and existed potential harm. Hence, we designed a ratiometric fluorescence probe (N,P@CDs@Eu) based on Eu functionalized nitrogen and phosphorus elements in-situ self-doping carbon dots (CDs) for detection of TC. The N,P@CDs was synthesized utilizing adenosine triphosphate (ATP) contained a significant amount of phosphate groups. These phosphate groups served as binding sites for Eu, facilitating functionalization of N,P@CDs to form the N,P@CDs@Eu. Under ultraviolet irradiation, N,P@CDs@Eu only exhibited the intense emission at 440 nm. However, during the sensing process, TC quenched the fluorescence at 440 nm of via inner filter effect (IFE), and induced N,P@CDs@Eu to exhibited fluorescence at 617 nm through antenna effect (AE). So that, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detection of TC had been constructed, which could achieve quantitative determination within 0.1–50 μM range, the detection limit of 22.6 nM. Moreover, N,P@CDs@Eu exhibited outstanding detection capabilities of TC in milk and tap water, with high recovery rates (99.47 %–108.12 %) and low errors (R < 4.68 %), indicating N,P@CDs@Eu had enormous application prospects in the field of food safety and environmental detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since its discovery in 1940 s, tetracycline (TC) has garnered significant attention due to cost-efficient, easy-production and excellent performance, and has been large-scale used in animal husbandry and fisheries [1,2]. However, the abuse of TC is frequent nowadays, which may lead to serious issues, including enhancing the antimicrobial resistance of the bacteria [3], contaminating water resources [4], endangering food safety, and posing threats to human health [5]. Therefore, developing a method with the properties of high-selectivity, high-sensitivity and high-efficiency to detect TC residues is imminent.

At present, a series of detection techniques have been extensively apply to the field of TC detection, such as colorimetric method [6], electrochemical analysis [7], chem-iluminiscence [8], chromatographic analysis [9]. However, above mentioned methods may exist various disadvantages of sophisticated equipment, high cost and complex operation, thus hindering their wide application. On the contrary, fluorescent measurement has properties of simple operation, fast response time, high sensitivity, and high selectivity, making it competent for the detection of TC under complex conditions [10–12]. So far, abundant “single-signal” fluorescence probes have been used to detect TC [13–15]. However, the “single-signal” fluorescence probes are susceptible to light source, pH value, temperature, apparatus etc. [16]. By contrast, the ratiometric fluorescence probes possess dual-signal sensing mechanisms of variation in fluorescence intensity, can effectively enhance measure accuracy and reduce the interference caused by external factors [17,18]. Moreover, the wavelength of light emitted by such fluorescence probes is within the visible spectrum range, which means that the visual detection of TC can be realized.

To date, there has been many fluorescence probes utilizing lanthanide ions to detect TC, such as GQDs-Eu3+ ratiometric probe [19], Eu/CdTe QDs ratiometric probe [20], DPA-Ce-GMP-Eu ratiometric probe [21]. Although TC has the β-diketone structure, which able to induce Eu3+ luminescence effectively via antena effect (AE), but the O–H vibrations in aqueous solvents will cause fluorescence quenching of Eu-TC chelate compound [22]. In addition, by selecting ancillary ligands as chelating agents to display water molecules though coordinate with Eu3+, which can impede coordination reaction between water molecules and the Eu-TC chelate complex to avoid fluorescence quenching [23]. However, it’s difficult to set up a ratiometric fluorescence probe for accurate TC detection by using ancillary ligand. Therefore, it is a necessity to bind another splendid signal as a response or reference signal to build a ratiometric fluorescence probe for TC detection.

Notably, with the superiorities of excellent fluorescence, splendid stability, aqueous solubility, hypotoxicity, and particular biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention in the field of sensors. In addition, by doping CDs with heteroatom, adjusting the intrinsic structure and improving quantum yield of CDs could be achieved easily [24–26]. Until now, researchers establish various nonmetallic elements doped CDs, principally including Si, B, F, S, N, P which are extensively used in the application field of fluorescent sensors. However, “single-signal” CDs is also easily affected by external factors, thereby researchers design functional CDs for detection TC, such as Zn-CDs [27], Eu-CDs [28], Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu [29], and CDs@ZSM-5:Eu3+ [30]. In short, CDs synthesized by different carbon sources may carry amino groups, hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups, and other groups. Above groups can efficaciously reduce cytotoxicity, enhance quantum yield, improve stability, and increase photostability of CDs in aqueous solution. Furthermore, carboxyl groups on CDs can as functionalization site to further chelate with lanthanide metal [29,31]. Hence, we can utilize heteroatom-carring carbon sources to synthetize in-situ heteroatoms self-doping CDs. The strategy can not only improve fluorescent property and sensing sensitivity of CDs, but also provide abundant reaction sites for further functionalization.

Herein, a novel flourescence probe N,P@CDs was synthesized through a facile and environmental-friendly procedure by utilizing adenosine triphosphate (ATP) as carbon source (Scheme 1 ). Compared with other nitrogen and phosphorus elements doped CDs obtained by adding other materials, the N,P@CDs@Eu designed in this paper only needed ATP as the carbon source to complete nitrogen and phosphorus elements in-situ self-doping carbon dots. The amino and phosphate groups carried in the ATP structure could effectively improve the luminescence of CDs, also provided abundant reaction sites for modifying Eu. Then, Eu was modified on the surface of N,P@CDs via post-modification synthesis method to get the N,P@CDs@Eu complex. Druing the TC detection process, TC could bind to Eu and sensitize by antenna effect (AE) to exhibit fluorescence at 617 nm, while the fluorescence of N,P@CDs@Eu was quenched on account of inner filter effect (IFE). Hence, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with outstanding sensitivity and selectivity was built by combined in-situ heteroatoms self-doping CDs and lanthanide metal, enabling effective detection of TC in practical samples.

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP, 95 %), Europium Nitrate (Eu(NO3)3, 99.9 %), tetracycline (TC, 98 %), Oxytetracycline (OTC, 98 %), Doxycycline (DOC, 98 %), Chlortetracycline (CTC, 98 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99 %), L-Tyrosine (Tyr, 99 %), Glutathione (Glu, 98 %), Glutathione (GSH), L-histidine (His, 99 %), L-Valine (Val, 98 %), L-Lysine (Lys, 98 %), DL-Proline (Pro, 98 %), L-Leucine (Leu, 98 %), chloramphenicol (CAP, 98 %), erythromycin (ERY, 98 %), kanamycin (KAN, 98 %), neomycin (NEO, 97 %), streptomycin sulfate (STR, 95 %).

The N,P@CDs were synthesized reference a previous paper [32]. First, 1 g ATP were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water. After stirring continuously for 1 h, above solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 220 ℃ heated for 6 h. After the reaction, the products were purified by centrifugation to remove large particles and were further filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, the purified N,P@CDs solution was dialyzed in a MD34 dialysis bag (1000 Da MWCO) for 24 h. The final N,P@CDs solution was saved at 4℃ in the dark.

The N,P@CDs@Eu was construed following the chelation reaction of Eu3+ with N and O element on the surface of N,P@CDs. Specifically, 1 mL N,P@CDs solution were respectively put into 4 mL Eu3+ aqueous solution (4, 8, 12, 14, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36 and 40 mM). Then, all the mixtures were stirred at room temperature for 12 h to obtain N,P@CDs@Eu. Finally, all the gained N,P@CDs@Eu solution were saved at 4℃.

Blend N,P@CDs@Eu solution (100 μL) and TC solution and dilution the mixture to 2 mL using Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0). TC detection was tested in after 40 s of incubation in room temperature, the fluorescence spectra of N,P@CDs@Eu + TC were measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and were recorded.

The N,P@CDs@Eu ratiometric fluorescence probe was applied to TC detection in real samples that may contain TC residues. The pure milk was carried out to get rid of the proteins and lipids in according to a previous method [23]. Then, used Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0) to dilute the pretreated milk and tap water to ten times, filtrated by 0.22 μm membrane. Using standard addition method, tap water samples and milk samples possessing various concentrations of TC (0, 5, 10, 20 μM) were prepared and used for fluorescence and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) measurement. The details of HPLC detection parameters were presented in the supporting information.

ATP is an important energy source that supports cell activity, which is made up of adenine, ribose and phosphate groups [33]. The CDs formed by ATP as a carbon source contains rich N and P elements. The N and P elements in self-doping CDs can enhance the fluorescence properties of N,P@CDs, and provide abundant reaction sites for modifying Eu. Finally, the N,P@CDs@Eu sensor was capable of realizing highly sensitive TC detection was fabricated through coordination reaction.

The morphology of N,P@CDs and N,P@CDs@Eu was explored by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As indicated in Fig. 1 a, the N,P@CDs possessed a good monodispersity and existed as a spheriform morphology with the approximate diameter was 15 nm. Compared with N,P@CDs, the morphology and diameter of N,P@CDs@Eu had no significant changed after Eu modified on the surface of N,P@CDs (Fig. 1b). And it was observed that N,P@CDs had a lattice spacing of about 0.216 nm (Fig. S1). And the lattice spacing of N,P@CDs could be distinctly observed, and it was 0.216 nm (Fig. S1). In addition, N,P@CDs exhibited an unusual polycrystalline (Fig. S2), where the diffraction peak at 2θ = 45.35° correspond to graphite lattice spacing of N,P@CDs. Based on previously reported CDs, we could speculate that the polycrystalline nature of N,P@CDs may be caused by phosphorus doping [34].

In addition, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the N,P@CDs and N,P@CDs@Eu were analyzed (Fig. 2 ). The infrared absorption peaks of C=N in N,P@CDs and N,P@CDs@Eu were shown at 1650 cm−1 and 1651 cm−1 [35], and an absorption peak belonging to N–H could be clearly observed in the 3000–4000 cm−1 [36]. The absorption peaks at 1110 cm−1 and 1254 cm−1, correspond to P-O and P=O of phosphate group in N,P@CDs, respectively [37]. In the infrared spectrum of N,P@CDs@Eu, the peak of P=O had obvious redshift effect, from 1254 cm−1 to 1286 cm−1, due to the coordination of Eu3+. These results certified the formation of N,P@CDs@Eu complex.

To investigate the binding mode between N,P@CDs and Eu, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of N,P@CDs and N,P@CDs@Eu were analyzed. The results were shown in the Fig. 2b–f, the C 1s-orbital, N 1s-orbital, O 1s-orbital, and P 2p-orbital could obviously find from the full range XPS spectrum of N,P@CDs. In the XPS survey spectrum of N,P@CDs@Eu, except peaks of C 1s-orbital, N 1s-orbital, O 1s-orbital, and P 2p-orbital, could observe the additional Eu 3d characteristic peak. In addition, three peaks at 287.57 eV, 285.81 eV, and 284.80 eV had decomposed from the C 1s XPS spectrum of N,P@CDs, attributing to C=O, C–N and C–C/C=C, respectively [38]. The N 1s XPS spectrum could be decomposed three peaks at 401.39 eV (N–C=C), 400.11 eV (C=N) and 399.08 eV (N–H) [32,39]. The deconvolution peaks at 532.66 eV and 531.57 eV could attribute to P=O and P-O in O 1s, respectively [40]. The P 2p XPS spectrum could divid into two peaks at 133.87 eV (P=O) and 133.16 eV (P–O) [41]. After Eu3+ modified on the surface of N,P@CDs, the deconvolution peaks of N 1s, O 1s and P 2p had the significant electron binding energy shift (Fig. S3). The N 1s XPS spectra of N,P@CDs@Eu were decomposed three peaks of N-C=C (402.22 eV), C=N (401.47 eV) and N–H (400.91 eV). The binding energy of deconvolution peaks in N,P@CDs@Eu were increases over N,P@CDs. These change was attributed to Eu-N coordination, causing the binding energy of N 1s-orbital to increase [42]. Compared with N,P@CDs, except peaks of P=O (532.72 eV) and P-O (531.95 eV) in the O 1s XPS spectra of N,P@CDs@Eu, could observe the additional peak at 530.80 eV belong to Eu-O bond [16,43]. After functionalization, the binding energy of P=O and P-O bonds shifted to higher values. These shifts were mainly due to the decrease of electron cloud density around O atom caused by the Eu-O coordination [44,45]. The deconvolution peaks of P 2p were shift to 135.07 eV (P=O) and 133.76 eV (P-O). The variation in the energy of the P 2p-orbital of phosphate groups attributed to the induction effect caused by the O atom linked to it [46]. The above results were attributed to Eu3+ form coordinate bonds with N and O on the unsaturated site in N,P@CDs.

The fluorescence properties of the N,P@CDs@Eu sensor were analyzed through UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As seen in Fig. 3 , N,P@CDs@Eu had an UV − vis absorption peak at 230 nm, which could attribute to the π–π* transitions of C=C or C=N bonds [47]. Moreover, a intense emission peak was observed at 440 nm under optimal excitation at 350 nm. As anticipated, the fluorescence at 440 nm was quenched during sensing TC, while TC could concurrently induce fluorescence emission at 617 nm of Eu3+, the result indicated N,P@CDs@Eu could apply to TC detection as a ratiometric fluorescence probe.

Optical properties of material was one of the important indexes to evaluate the excellence of the sensor. For ensuring the optical performance of N,P@CDs@Eu, optimized reaction conditions of the functionalization procedure of N,P@CDs (Fig. S4). Eu3+ occurred protonation and precipitation phenomena under conditions of excess acid and excess base, leading to a decrease in the Eu loading amount [23]. During the functionalization process, more Eu was loaded onto the N,P@CDs surface when the reaction pH was 8.0, resulting in N,P@CDs@Eu + TC reached the maximum value of F617/F440. Besides, the loading amount of Eu was relevant for CEu 3+ during the reaction. F617/F440 increased with CEu 3+ within a certain concentration range, and reached the peak value when CEu 3+ was 28 mM. With a view to the sensing sensitivity, the reaction conditions for N,P@CDs functionalization procedure was determined to be pH 8.0 and CEu 3+=28 mM.

To take full advantage of the N,P@CDs@Eu towards TC sensing capabilities, it was imperative to investigate the optimal conditions of the sensing process. As shown in the Fig. S5, as the pH value of the sensing system increased from 4.0 to 9.0, F617/F440 in the sensing process also increased due to the protonation degree of Eu3+ was weakened. However, Eu3+ precipitated in the solution at pH > 9.0, leading to a decrease in F617/F440, indicating the optimum sensing condition to be pH 9.0. Furthermore, F617/F440 stabilized after the addition of TC into N,P@CDs@Eu solution for 40 s, indicating the response to TC has been completed, so confirming incubation time being 40 s.

Following the establishment of the optimal sensing conditions for N,P@CDs@Eu to TC, a more in-depth exploration of its sensing performance would be pursued. We documented the fluorescence emission spectra of N,P@CDs@Eu in response to during 0–60 μM TC. The fluorescence intensity at 440 nm gradually diminished with increasing TC concentration, while the fluorescence intensity at 617 nm steadily intensified (Fig. 4 a). Plotting the coordinates with F617/F440 as the ordinate, and performing linear fitting, displaying a satisfactory linear correlation between F617/F440 in the 0.1–50 μM concentration of TC range (F617/F440 = 0.00682CTC–0.00451, R2 = 0.9948) with the LOD of 22.6 nM (Fig. 4b).
LOD = 3σ/k(1)
where σ represented the standard deviation of 11 consecutive determinations of blank samples, and k represented the slope of the standard curve.

Compared with the different types of fluorescence sensors, N,P@CDs@Eu exhibited the superior sensing property (Table S1). Moreover, under UV irradiation, the optical color of N,P@CDs@Eu were obviously changing from blue to pink with adding the TC (Fig. 4c), indicating the N,P@CDs@Eu had significant potential for the visual of TC detection.

In order to assess the selectivity and anti-interference of the N,P@CDs@Eu sensor in application, the responses of other interfering substance to the probe were recorded (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Tyr, Glu, Leu, Pro, Val, Lys, His, CAP, NEO, KAN, STR, ERY, GSH). With the exception of the TCs (OTC, DOC, CTC), other interfering substances did not affect the sensing performance of N,P@CDs@Eu (Fig. 5 and Fig. S6), indicating N,P@CDs@Eu had superior selectivity and anti-interference. As illustrated in Fig. S7, TCs encompassed analogous structures, and could led to fluorescence alterations of N,P@CDs@Eu. Hence, N,P@CDs@Eu could be employed for sensing TCs. Given its wider application in the animal husbandry, we had chosen TC as a representative to analyze the performance of N,P@CDs@Eu. Moreover, its fluorescence intensity decreased by less than 10 % after the solution (N,P@CDs@Eu and N,P@CDs@Eu + TC) was placed for 5 h (Fig. S8), indicating N,P@CDs@Eu had superior stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting the sensing mechanism of TC
ce:section.ce:para:
Eu3+ was a lanthanide metal with possessed f-f forbidden transitions [50] and could emit light through the antenna effect (AE) [30]. Eu3+ was anchored by N and O on the N,P@CDs, and the excess water molecules will coordinate with Eu3+ on this unsaturated coordination mode. At this point, the vibration of –OH in the water molecule would cause the energy to dissipate in a non-radiative manner. In theory, TC could utilize its β-diketone structure to displace water molecules and form a strong coordination with Eu3+, resulting in sensitized the fluorescence emission of Eu3+ [51]. To confirm the hypothesis, we tested UV–vis absorption spectra of various systems (Fig. 6c). The absorption peak of TC at 370 nm generated redshift to 400 nm with addition of the N,P@CDs@Eu, confirming the formation of N,P@CDs@Eu + TC complex. Furthermore, we computed the binding constant of N,P@CDs@Eu and TC utilizing the modified Benesi-Hilderbrand equation [52].
[F∞-F0]/[FX-F0] = 1 + 1/K[Q](2)
where F∞ and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of the N,P@CDs@Eu solution in the absence of TC and at a concentration of complete response, respectively; FX was the fluorescence intensity under the condition of adding Q concentration TC and K represented the binding constant.

Recorded the fluorescence intensity of N,P@CDs@Eu at 617 nm under varying concentrations of TC and combine the modified Benesi-Hilderbrand equation to get the curve as shown in the Fig. S9. The binding constant was 3.15 × 104 M−1. In Fig. 6d, TC had the energy gap (ΔEg=S1-T1) over 5000 cm−1 between the singlet excited-state energy (S1 = 36,200 cm−1) and triplet excited-state energy (T1 = 19,160 cm−1), and T1 of TC had more energy than 5D0 (17,500 cm−1) of Eu3+ [31]. Therefore, energy transfer through TC to Eu3+ was an efficient process that enhanced the fluorescence emission intensity of Eu3+ at 617 nm.

In order to evaluate the possibility of detection TC in real-life scenarios, N,P@CDs@Eu had been used to detect residual TC in practical samples. Exhibited in the Table 2 , N,P@CDs@Eu did not detect the presence of TC in actual samples. Further testing using the standard addition method yielded the recovery rate of 99.47 %–106.51 % and relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3.54 %, which was no significant difference observed when compared to the results obtained from the high-performance liquid chromatography method (p < 0.05). The aforementioned tests indicated N,P@CDs@Eu was capable of providing a reliable assessment of TC levels in actual samples, exhibiting promising prospects for practical application.

In summary, a novel lanthanide-based sensing system, the ratiometric fluorescent probe (N,P@CDs@Eu) had been successfully synthesized for detection TC with speediness and high sensitivity. Under the combined influence of IFE and AE, the blue fluorescence (λem = 440 nm) of N,P@CDs@Eu was quenched by TC, while the fluorescence (λem = 617 nm) of Eu was sensitized. Especially the detection of TC could be quickly completed within 40 s, and LOD was as low as 22.6 nM. With milk and tap water as real samples, TC could be accurately detected with high recovery rates (99.47 %–108.12 %) and low errors (R < 4.68 %), proving its practicality in the field of food detection and environmental monitoring.

Weifeng Hou: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation. Chuyao Liang: Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis. Yi Li: Validation, Supervision, Investigation. Cuicui Shao: Validation, Supervision, Investigation. Yanghuan Zheng: Supervision, Investigation. Zhijian Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shouzhi Pu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Congbin Fan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22205085, 22261020 and 22263005), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20242BAB25147 and 20224ACB203006), Science and Technology Project of Jiangxi Provincial Education Department (GJJ2202501), the youth talent Fund project of Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University (2022QNBJRC006). Suported by Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules (No: 2024SSY05141). The authors would like to thank the shiyanjia lab (www. shiyanjia.com) for the XPS test.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2025.116411.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An upconversion nanosensor for rapid and sensitive detection of tetracycline in food based on magnetic-field-assisted separation
Food Chem.
